questions.text: The ability of an organism to produce new individuals of its own kind is called:
questions.explanation: Reproduction is a fundamental biological process by which existing organisms produce offspring.

questions.text: Amoeba reproduces by which method of asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: In binary fission, a single cell divides into two equal halves.

questions.text: Hydra reproduces by using regenerative cells for the process of:
questions.explanation: Hydra produces an outgrowth (bud) which develops into a new individual and detaches from the parent.

questions.text: The basic event in reproduction is the creation of a:
questions.explanation: DNA copying is essential to transfer genetic information from parents to offspring.

questions.text: Planaria can be cut into any number of pieces and each piece grows into a complete organism. This is:
questions.explanation: Regeneration is the ability of a fully differentiated organism to give rise to new individual organisms from its body parts.

questions.text: Spirogyra reproduces by breaking up into smaller pieces upon maturation. This process is:
questions.explanation: Multicellular organisms with relatively simple body organization, like Spirogyra, simply break up into smaller pieces.

questions.text: Bryophyllum reproduces vegetatively through its:
questions.explanation: Buds produced in the notches along the leaf margins of Bryophyllum fall on the soil and develop into new plants.

questions.text: The thread-like structures that develop on bread (Rhizopus) are called:
questions.explanation: The hyphae are non-reproductive parts; spores are produced in the sporangia for reproduction.

questions.text: Which of the following is a disadvantage of asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction produces clones, meaning there is no genetic diversity to help adapt to changing environments.

questions.text: Sexual reproduction involves the fusion of:
questions.explanation: Fusion of male and female gametes (fertilization) results in a zygote with a new combination of DNA.

questions.text: The male reproductive part of a flower is the:
questions.explanation: The stamen consists of the anther (which produces pollen) and the filament.

questions.text: The female reproductive part located in the center of a flower is the:
questions.explanation: The carpel consists of the ovary, style, and stigma.

questions.text: Transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma is called:
questions.explanation: Pollination can be self-pollination or cross-pollination.

questions.text: After fertilization, the zygote divides many times to form an embryo within the:
questions.explanation: The ovule eventually develops a tough coat and is converted into a seed.

questions.text: The ovary of a flower grows rapidly and ripens to form the:
questions.explanation: While ovules become seeds, the ovary becomes the fruit.

questions.text: The period of adolescence when reproductive tissues begin to mature is called:
questions.explanation: Puberty involves physical and hormonal changes leading to sexual maturity.

questions.text: In human males, the testes are located outside the abdominal cavity in the scrotum because:
options: Sperm formation requires a lower temperature than body temperature
questions.explanation: Sperm production is sensitive to heat and needs a temperature 2-3°C lower than normal body temperature.

questions.text: Which hormone is responsible for changes in boys during puberty?
questions.explanation: Testosterone regulates sperm production and brings about secondary sexual characteristics in males.

questions.text: In human females, eggs are produced in the:
questions.explanation: Ovaries produce one egg every month and also secrete hormones like estrogen and progesterone.

questions.text: Fertilization in humans usually occurs in the:
questions.explanation: The sperm meets the egg in the oviduct to form a zygote.

questions.text: The embryo gets nutrition from the mother's blood with the help of a special tissue called:
questions.explanation: The placenta is a disc-like tissue embedded in the uterine wall that facilitates exchange of nutrients and waste.

questions.text: The time period from fertilization to the birth of the baby is called:
questions.explanation: In humans, the gestation period is approximately nine months.

questions.text: If the egg is not fertilized, the thick lining of the uterus breaks and comes out through the vagina as blood and mucus. This is:
questions.explanation: Menstruation occurs roughly every 28 days and lasts for about 2-8 days.

questions.text: Which of the following is a bacterial Sexually Transmitted Disease (STD)?
questions.explanation: Gonorrhoea and Syphilis are bacterial infections, while AIDS and Warts are viral.

questions.text: Copper-T is a contraceptive device placed in the:
questions.explanation: Intrauterine Contraceptive Devices (IUCDs) like Copper-T prevent pregnancy by blocking implantation.

questions.text: Surgical blocking of the Fallopian tube in females is called:
questions.explanation: Tubectomy prevents the egg from reaching the uterus, thus preventing fertilization.

questions.text: Surgical blocking of the Vas Deferens in males is called:
questions.explanation: Vasectomy prevents the transfer of sperm.

questions.text: A flower containing both stamens and carpels is called:
questions.explanation: Examples of bisexual flowers include Hibiscus and Mustard.

questions.text: Leishmania reproduces through binary fission in a definite orientation because of its:
questions.explanation: Leishmania, which causes kala-azar, has a whip-like structure at one end, dictating the plane of fission.

questions.text: The male germ-cell (sperm) consists of mainly genetic material and a:
questions.explanation: The long tail helps the sperm to move towards the female germ-cell.

questions.text: Variation is useful for the survival of ______ over time.
questions.explanation: Variation allows species to adapt to environmental changes, even if some individuals perish.

questions.text: Plasmodium, the malarial parasite, divides into many daughter cells simultaneously by:
questions.explanation: Multiple fission is an asexual method where one cell produces many offspring at once.

questions.text: Tissue culture is a technique used to grow new plants from ______ of the parent plant.
options: Cells or tissues from the growing tip
questions.explanation: Tissue culture allows for rapid propagation of disease-free plants in artificial media.

questions.text: Commonly used method for growing jasmine or rose plants is:
questions.explanation: These are artificial methods of vegetative propagation used in agriculture and horticulture.

questions.text: The part of the seed that contains the future plant is the:
questions.explanation: The embryo develops into a seedling under appropriate conditions (germination).

questions.text: The part of the seed that stores food is the:
questions.explanation: Cotyledons store nutrients for the developing embryo.

questions.text: Which part of the male reproductive system carries sperms from the testes to the urethra?
questions.explanation: The vas deferens is the tube that transports mature sperm.

questions.text: Glands like the prostate and seminal vesicles add their secretions to the sperms to:
options: Make transport easier and provide nutrition
questions.explanation: These fluids form semen, providing a liquid medium for sperm mobility and survival.

questions.text: The female reproductive system is designed to:
options: Carry and nurture the developing child
questions.explanation: It produces germ cells, facilitates fertilization, and supports pregnancy.

questions.text: Condoms are a type of ______ barrier for contraception.
questions.explanation: Mechanical barriers prevent the sperm from reaching the egg and also help prevent the transmission of STDs.
